speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. Judy, what effect has the Holocaust had on decisions you've made since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one big decision was that I wanted to work with children. I wanted to do some sort of work with children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm, I'm sure I didn't process it at the time. I wanted to have a position of helping children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Out of necessity of having a quick means of earning money when we came to America, I became a dental hygienist. My uncle was a dentist. He was a role model once we moved here. I think I was always looking for a father role model. And my mother's brothers were very-- how should we say-- they were good about taking on this responsibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he suggested that a dental hygienist would be a good profession for a woman, because it wouldn't be a heavy investment into equipment. Because I was talking about becoming a dentist at the time. It was only a two-year period of training. And so I became a dental hygienist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, when I had more choices after, you know, being a wife and mom only for quite a period, I decided I wanted to go back to school and learn to treat children with their problems, which is probably-- you know, that's coming all the way around. Maybe I felt that by treating other children, it would help me. We all pick our professions for various reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I finally then sort of gravitated toward doing social work with children. And I'm doing it now in a hospital. And it may be that is related to some of the hospitalizations I had in the, in this camp period. I mean, maybe there is some-- it just occurs to me-- there might be some undercurrent that made me go back to treating children in hospitals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I get-- not always-- but it makes me feel good to do it. And sometimes it's very difficult work. But on the other hand then-- and, and, and in the beginning when I did it, I thought I'd make big differences. And I learned that you can only make little differences. But at this point, I'm already happy when I can make a little difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it otherwise influence my life? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I think I have become a forced optimist. You know, I want to look for, for good days. I want to look for-- not, not having fun anymore, but for having enjoyment out of what the positive things that life has to offer. I love being a grandmother. I mean, it's, it's just wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- you know, I, I have a, shall we say, almost a hunger for-- not material things of life, although that feels good, too, but for, you know, the joys of, of nature, and plants, and, and, you know, the things that make you feel good. And I think that may very well have come from that period. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I hope that answers it. Is there anything you'd like to add to the-- what you've said so far?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think basically, I want my children to know who I am. And maybe this is one way of conveying this to them. Maybe that's easier for me than talking directly. I feel that it's extremely important that the world knows about this, not just me. I mean, this whole project-- I think that that testimony is given. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At one time, I met Elie Wiesel. And he wrote in-- I had bought a book of, of his-- And he wrote in there, 'To Judy Straus, who seeks, who remembers.' And maybe at that point, I started deciding yes, I do remember. And, and now I feel that all those who remember must speak before they're gone. I mean, one of these days, we're going to be gone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the things that really hurt me during this period was the-- what I consider the betrayal of one Jew against another in, in not that important things-- I mean, maybe my doll. But I saw that as a, as a betrayal. The fact that the Dutch Jews, and the German Jews, and the Czech Jews didn't get along, I saw that as almost, in a way, more painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see of that as a child in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just heard it. I just could hear it in, in the way they were talking. Did I see actual ostracizing? No. But I, I could hear it. In fact, I met somebody not that long ago, maybe five years ago, a Czech Jew who I said I was in the same camp that you were in. And he said something-- was I German? I said, no. You know, I said I was Dutch. I somehow or other denied my being born in Germany for my-- whatever their reasons were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, oh, I'm glad. I thought you were German. And I thought, what's the difference? You know, who cares? You know, why this division? We had one enemy. Why each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so this part bothers me, you see. But unfortunately, I've learned. You see this in every place that people are connected in a common thing, even in a hospital. There's politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to expect Jewish people to, under urgent condition, to drop that is maybe unrealistic. But as a child, I just-- that's the more painful part of the camp period-- not, not the food, you know, not the hygiene, not, not some of the daily stuff. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that sort of expresses, you know, my feelings. And I find it very important to-- I, I think I probably am trying to be a bridge maker in, in, in, in a lot of human situations-- you know, in the hospital, in the-- I, I just don't feel that people should be divided. And I might be a little bit too rose-colored about that. Because maybe I just can't stand the divisions. So, OK? Could you tell me about your necklace, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We got some of the things back after the war that were not the best that was hidden, so to speak. The-- where the crystal, and the good dishes, and all that went-- we didn't get that back. It went underground, and it just disappeared. My mother gave some stuff that wasn't as much value to what used to be our maid. And we got that back. And there were all these odd pieces of gold that belonged to my father, and my grandmother, and my grandfather. And I just decided. I had it all melted down. And there is a lady here in Chicago who creates a new thing out of it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's where this necklace comes from. And it gives me a certain amount of good feeling to wear it. And this may be very much related to everybody needing some-- something concrete to remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of us survivors do not go to graves. There aren't any. And somehow or other, when I wear this necklace-- and that's why I put it on. I feel that my family's with me. OK? Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my paternal grandmother. She lived in a small town called [INAUDIBLE], And I know actually very little about her, because they did not-- I mean, she died in the beginning of the war of, of old age.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanchen Levy. And I don't know her maiden name. This is my maternal grandmother. Her name was Fina Falkenstein. She was a born Jonas. It-- the picture might have been taken in Holland maybe when she brought me, Because I remember the chair. And so either my mother had this chair brought from Germany, or this picture was taken in Holland. She was a very important figure for me, because although I don't remember it, she-- I spent the first year and a half with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in probably '37-- '36 or '37. And she had kidney failure. This is my grandfather, Moses, from-- maternal grandfather. He came to Holland in the late '30s. And he lived with us for a while. And I have some very strong attachments to him, because he used to spend time with me when nobody else had time. And I used to sit on his lap. And I used to comb his hair. I remember that. And he apparently allowed me to do things that he never allowed his own children to do. Because I got some comments from my mother about that. He was very loving. And I spent a lot of time with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in Sobibor in 1943. He was taken to Westerbork and immediately, I heard later, transported to Sobibor. And he died when he was 75 years old. But I have very good memories of him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a gold watch, which belonged to my grandfather. And, obviously, I have it. And it had-- I'm glad I have it. It has great meaning for me. One of the ways that he used to sort of quiet me down, or, or calm me down, or steady me, or whatever you want to call that, is he would pull that out of his pocket, and open it up, and let me listen to it. And it would tick. And this watch is now 95 years old. And it still ticks. And I think that my uncle had it. And then when he died, he gave it to me. That's the uncle who was hidden. And I, I value it. I, I want to have it. I have it in my room under a dome. And it's an important piece for me. And so it opens up. And then you-- then what he used to do is he would then put it to my ear. We had this ritual. And then I'd hear the ticking. And there was a certain steadiness in that. And it's still there. This is a picture of my mother and her three brothers. And they were all really very important to me in, in my life. The, the, the man with the glasses, with a dark-rimmed glasses, his name was Ludwig. But I called him Dada. And he was a lawyer. And he's the one who was hidden in Germany and-- in Holland during the war. And he had a, a disability, so he could never come to the United States. The, the, the taller gentleman in the-- is my Uncle Julius, [? Uhler. ?] He came to America before the war. He started his business with one cow in a shed and then became a meat packer and went-- you know, his business grew very nicely. The-- then there is my mother. And then there is my Uncle Eric, who is-- was a dentist. And he also-- all three of them had a big influence on, on how I was raised and, and on my memories. This is a picture of my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have good memories of him, but they aren't-- how shall we say-- they aren't so much in, in raising. It's just some good feelings of being together with him. I used to go and inspect his business with him on, on Sundays. He was 11 years older than my mother, and I think that it showed. In, in-- I remember in Theresienstadt, my, my mother said to my father-- when he had to go on transport-- I want you to wear an overall, so that they can see right away that you know how to work. But he was pale. And he had lost weight. And we've been told that he did not survive selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly. I don't have that many pictures of my father. I would presume it was taken in the beginning of the war sometime. And this is a picture of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was a very charming woman. She was a typical European product, the way females are raised in those days. And she followed whatever, you know, my father said was, was necessary, except during the camp time. She became all of a sudden the one who-- at least the way it appears to me-- who really pulled things off, who organized things, who, who almost dictated to my father what he had to do. And then after after war, it just went like a balloon. And she was never the same again. But-- and, and she got sick very early after the war already. But I have good memories of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1973 at the age of 70. She had many strokes-- the first one when she was 53 years old. And it was sort of degenerating health after that. She was really-- after the war, she was-- I don't think she was ever happy again, maybe with the grandchildren. This is a picture of my Uncle Ludwig-- and I have only called him Dada, and so did everybody else-- and his wife when they were married somewhere in the early '50s, I think. I think he was the person most influential over how I was raised, because he sort of guided my mother and me after the war. And we lived with him. He was a very, very fine person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was [? Rowe ?] [? Frankfurt. ?] And then she became a Falkenstein. And she's a survivor of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is your uncle still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my uncle died of an illness that he's had all his life that caused his disabilities. And he died in 1967.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife died a couple years later. But we had a good relationship with her. And you know, she came and visited in America. And I still have a relationship with her son. We just saw him last May. And he lives in, in Holland, so-- This is a picture of my school class after we were forced to have Jewish schools and, and non-Jewish schools. But it was before we had to wear stars. Because you don't see any stars on clothes yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken probably in '41 or '42. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are, as far as I know, only two people living out of this class. And typical of this-- of the way things were dealt with in those days, if there was an empty seat the next day after there was a razia-- what, what they were called-- that was not addressed by the teacher. But I went to this school until June of '93-- or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or '43

speaker: interviewee
text: May of '9--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of the photos are photo albums, actually, my mother had with the, with the cleaning, with the, with the maid. And we did get these back. This is a report card, believe it or not, which is from Westerbork. At one point, they started to have school again. And we had classes. We had all the regular subjects. The teachers were really dedicated, at least-- there was this controversy with the German counterpart of the school. But most of the teachers-- and I remember the one that starts with an L, [? Litner. ?] He was a very Zionist figure. And he spoke in great terms about-- glowing terms about Palestine and, and that we would all hope to, to go there. This one is signed by my father. And my parents went over these report cards with me in all seriousness, that this was important for them that I would be learning. So I have three report cards left from that period, which I think were in, in, in a book that I was able to have with me during this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the school day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I really don't remember the, the practical parts. I, I would presume that it was quite a bit longer than usual, because it was one way to get the kids out of the streets-- I mean, to have them in, in one place. So I think it probably was a very long school day. And it probably was a six-day school day. But I do not remember that. This is an identification [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we all had to carry with us, that we had to have. And it was-- it came from Westerbork. This picture is clipped. The original picture, I remember, had a star on it. And I remember the dress, too. But this was strictly an identification that everybody had to have to prove that they were who they said they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried it. It's-- the fold is almost torn, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept it through Theresienstadt as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There must have been some things in this one book that I was able to carry with me through the camps that-- because I don't know where else it would have come from. This is a ration card from Theresienstadt for the month of June. My number is on there. We all were given a number. And it's 247450. My name is on there, Ursel Levy, Levy. And in order to, you know, get your food in, in, in the, in the food kitchen, you have to present this card. And then they would cut out the date. Now this was already after liberation. And obviously, we didn't go to the kitchen all the time. We must have had some other sources of getting food then. But I just saved the card. The name Ursel-- when we came to America, I decided I was going to be called Judith, from [INAUDIBLE]. And I mean, in retrospect now, I think that it was just like I was forcing an identity change by taking on a different name that would, you know, fit right in with all the American Judys. So it's at that point that I became Judy in 1949. This is the star that I wore, that we had to wear. And it says the Dutch word for Jew, which is [? 'Yote.' ?] The letters, I think, we're purposefully designed to look like Hebrew letters, although they're not. And I have actually two stars. One of them is my mother's, and one is mine. And we, we had to wear this. You could not be caught without it. This is a handkerchief that used to belong to my mother that she obviously brought to the camp. Her name is on there-- Alma. And her transport number is on there. There must have been some way of laundering it. And it's actually the only piece of, let's say, clothing that I, that I have left over from then. What we used for handkerchiefs is every once in a while, they would hand out sanitary napkins. And we would split them into layers. And that became most people's handkerchiefs. And I remember that the very first time they were giving out these sanitary napkins, I stood in line. And when it was my turn, they wouldn't give me any. And I didn't even know what they were giving out. But I just felt that if anybody else was getting something, I had to have something, too. And I remember being very incensed about being skipped. But anyway, this is-- this was my mother's handkerchief in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she able to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It's somehow or other something that I either found in her belongings after she died-- I think that's where, where I probably got it. Because I don't remember the exact source. This is a book that I, at one time, got as a present from my parents. And this book went with me through the camps. And, obviously, I still have it. The, the barrack that we were in, barrack 46D, is on there, that it was in Westerbork. And then I made a notation after the war. And what I wrote in this is sort of typical of, of, of my almost denial-- 'This book was given to me on the occasion of a good report card. I took it to W-- which is Westerbork-- and T-- Theresienstadt-- and then also again back home.' When I look at it now, I think it's, it's really typical that I wouldn't even spell the names of, of the camps out. This is a picture that was taken in probably in 1947 of me after the war. And that's the way I wanted to look at that time. This is a letter that I wrote to my American relatives in 1945. It's written in Dutch. And very typical of what I was in those days, I wrote, 'During the war I was in the camp. On the way to the camp, we passed a river, the Elbe. And on the way back, we passed another river, the Rhine.' And this is a picture of sort of of now. This was taken two years ago. The gentleman over on the left is our new son-in-law, Dan. Then comes our daughter, Susan, our youngest. Then comes daughter Lynn, who's our oldest, then her husband, Jim. Next to that is my husband of 40 years. Then comes granddaughter Allie. And in the meantime, we have another granddaughter whose name is Sarah. Then there is a picture of me. Then there's our son, David, who in the meantime, got married. And there is a new daughter whose name is Lynn Straus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, so much, Judy, for doing this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You're welcome.
